Title: DotCom Secrets: The Underground Playbook for Growing Your Company Online
Author: Russell Brunson
My Review:
The book has a bunch of images in each chapter that make it memorable. The final chapter mentions that even after finishing the book, the discussed ideas continue to roam in your mind rent-free. I’ll admit there’s truth to that. One can draw the 'secrets' from this book, almost unconsciously.

What I don’t agree with is the author’s claim that this book is forever relevant. The book repeatedly propagates the idea that internet marketing secrets will remain the same regardless of technological changes or circumstances. I cannot take that seriously when, in fact, some of the links inside the book appear dead. Not to mention, I cringed at the sales strategies in some parts.

Nevertheless, I still find that this is the best book for internet marketing and sales without always being 'salesy.' If you dig deeper, there is some form of wisdom in each and every line. Some of my favorite parts are about the three types of traffic (cold, warm, and hot) and how/when to think about each one. It confirmed my belief that most of the engagement (at least early-on in your business) and online traffic you get is pointless.

Title: The Millionaire Fast Lane: Crack the Code to Wealth and Live Rich for a Lifetime
Author: M.J. DeMarco
My Review:
The Millionaire Fastlane promotes itself as a mathematical guideline to make anyone rich, and it does the job reasonably well. I did make some drastic lifestyle changes in the process of reading this. Namely, pausing my ongoing Mutual Funds SIP and starting to market myself more on social media (X). And I think I'll continue to make some weird changes that may be influenced by this book in some way or the other. However, I'll suggest you to take the principles from this book with a pinch of salt and pepper because financial success is different for everyone.

This is also a long book, mostly interesting and relevant. The most important ideas I have found are about compounding, leverage, scale, time/freedom. All of which I have read or heard at least once elsewhere; here, everything is contexualized in a succinct way. MJ also has amazing storytelling, without much fluff or unecessary repetition.

MJ attempts to drill the concept of freedom in every possible way. About this... I am not sure how I feel about. The desire for (financial) freedom can be like a double-edged sword. Specifically, as the world seems to heading towards more passivity and longevity, the appeal of high-earners with a relaxed life may not be so valuable in the years to come. I may still need some more knowledge/experience to really put it into words but it's probably one thing that stops me from rating this a solid-5.

Title: The Sovereign Individual: Mastering the Transition to the Information Age
Author: James Dale Davidson
My Review:
The Sovereign Individual and Thinking, Fast and Slow are two of the longest books I’ve read this year. The common thing between these two is the outrageous amount of repetition of same ideas. Over and over again until you get tired. This brain feed may absolutely be the norm about 10-15 years ago when information and internet were expensive, but it’s a good experience nevertheless to manifest the ideas in this book repeatedly. It kind of gives you different ways of thinking about the thumb rule of politics: imposition of violence.

This is the best book I’ve read till date that combines politics, economics, and technology. I never thought that this is a readable combination. But here the economics and especially technology aspects made the politics (which I was probably never keen about until now) even more interesting.

The prominent idea I took away is that The Information Age shifts power (and violence) away from large nation-states and toward individuals and smaller, more agile entities. This idea was drummed by the author in different frequencies. Although I don’t agree with all the ideas, especially as this book is a little outdated, I appreciated in its entirety as I reached its end.

Title: Poor Charlie's Almanack: The Wit and Wisdom of Charles T. Munger, Expanded Third Edition
Author: Peter D. Kaufman

Title: Breath: The New Science of a Lost Art
Author: James Nestor
My Review:
Breath: The Science of Lost Art mostly talks about the importance of breathwork in our daily lives. The modern lifestyle, although better in many ways, breeds all kind of diseases. I think the core of this issue is our diet. High sugar diets leading to glucose spikes, which are chain reactions to many other negative health conditions. Notably, high blood pressure. While, low-quality and highly processed diets disconfigure your mouth in the long-term. This is one of the worst things to go unnoticed behind our backs, and affects your airway in horrible ways.

Chew hard food more often (preservative-free chewing gum works), increase protein and fat intake more than carbohydrates (prefer natural proteins over whey), reduce glucose-spiking foods (millets>rice). Only after you cross this threshold of good diet, proper breathing techniques may provide meaningful benefits.

Various breathing techniques are discussed in a satisfying depth. There is an Appendix at the end, which does better job at discerning how to use the techniques then the chapters themselves. The book is a good read for surrendering yourself though. It directly quotes the various discussions author had with professors, pulmonologist, heart doctors, breathing experts, etc. Again, the appendix is worth the dime for breathing enthusiasts!

Title: The Dip: A Little Book That Teaches You When to Quit (and When to Stick)
Author: Seth Godin
My Review:
The Dip happens when you feel like quitting, but you shouldn't. Continuing to put effort leads to non-linear results. There are other cases or domains of work where your judgement fails. Putting more effort leads to diminishing returns.

The book is pretty good and of the best short ones I have read. The main point at the end is to figure out when to quit. My best takeaway is stretegic quitting: pre-planning of the potential quitting situations before starting knowledge work rather than emotional (reactionary) quitting due to obstacles.

Title: Thinking, Fast and Slow
Author: Daniel Kahneman
My Review:
The book is like a cognitive guide with a bunch of relatable examples. It offers the notion of two brain systems (fast & slow) and expands it into every chapter. The narrative of the book gets more and more saggy at many times in the book, with lines that get you out of focus. However, towards the end of the book, you would likely feel grateful for having read through the detailed insights. The first part of the four parts is a great introduction to the two systems, and one of my favorite parts.

The second part emphasizes how human judgment is more story-driven than fact-based. People tend to prefer coherent narratives over statistical truths, often jumping to conclusions from small or unrepresentative samples. Like reading a book by its cover, the mind often fills in gaps with assumptions, not facts. Many statistics-math concepts like Bayesian thinking, Regression, and Law of Small Numbers are retold into really fresh contexts, making this part pretty interesting.

The third and fourth parts, with a total of sixteen chapters about Overconfidence and Choices, are my favorite ones. The intersection of availability, emotion, and risk. is unpacked. Probability neglect shows how vivid narratives and repeated exposure can inflate public fear and skew decision-making. The “availability cascade,” for example, is not just media hype—it’s how shared misperceptions become institutionalized errors. You noted this sharply, recognizing how System 1 can drive a social snowball of concern. You can kind of observe this all around you if you notice, especially today with a lot of news buzz.

The final (fifth) part about Life is the one I have skimmed through the most. Towards the end, the acuteness of the mind does overshadow the clutter, and is thus worth cutting through the fat of the book. Overall, a great book, and I will deeply reread selected parts from the book.
Private Notes: Overall great book, I will deeply reread selected parts from the book.

Title: The Intelligent Investor
Author: Benjamin Graham
My Review:
The Intelligent Investor makes it pretty clear that it is trying to be a mindset book. It reads like a narration and gets pretty complicated at some points. This is a kind of 'experiential theory' and needs effort in order to extract good ideas for current times.

The theory is actually where the book sometimes shines. The concepts in this book are not any kind of hard science, but, rather, reflects directly from the Benjamin's experiences. The concepts are little spread, a lot of which, at different places on the web. Reading the book is definitely better and direct than reading related articles or blogs online. Following are some of my favorite lines:

The intelligent investor realizes that stocks become more risky, not less, as their prices rise—and less risky, not more, as their prices fall.


Never mingle your speculative and investment operations in the same account, nor in any part of your thinking.


Our readers must have enough intelligence to recognize that even high-quality stocks cannot be a better purchase than bonds under all conditions—i.e., regardless of how high the stock market may be and how low the current dividend return compared with the rates available on bonds.


Anyone who panicked in the last bear market is going to panic in the next one—and will regret having no cushion of cash and bonds.


It is a mere statistical chore to identify companies that have “out-performed the averages” in the past.


You’ll never be able to boast that you beat the market, because the job of an index fund is to match the market’s return, not to exceed it.


I read the revised edition btw, so shoutout to the commentary section at the end of every chapter which is sometimes better than the chapter itself!

Title: The Dental Diet: The Surprising Link between Your Teeth, Real Food, and Life-Changing Natural Health
Author: Steven Lin
My Review:
This is a great book about teeth health. It takes a non-standard approach to create strong relations between oral health and other fields of medicine. And a great part of this book talks about how most of our modern diet choices suck. But the best thing I have learned from this book is about the importance of wholesome diet and use of teeth as biomarkers.
It is easier to deep dive into a field like dentistry by exploring connections with other branches. Epigenetics, for example, is the field of how your DNA molecules can be altered by surrounding environment like food we eat. When you get oral cavities, it is a good sign that something bad possibly can occur for you overall health as well. Similarly gut microbiome is a place with a much larger scale of reactions to the oral microbiome. So, you can assume gum health will likely cause pretty bad issues in your digestive tracts. It is interesting to look back to understand how the clear oral-condition signals can be dealt with more respect.

Title: How We Learn: Why Brains Learn Better Than Any Machine . . . for Now
Author: Stanislas Dehaene
My Review: Incredible science. At glance, 'How We Learn' seems a narrow topic. But its appeal is much broader. Each Chapter has an interesting list of sub-topics which require close attention. Every notion is related to the working of our brain. It's like trying to catch each thread of a cloth. And if you do it right, you’ll become more sensitive to learning. Begin the book and keep thinking.

Title: The Inevitable: Understanding the 12 Technological Forces That Will Shape Our Future
Author: Kevin Kelly
My Review:
“I mean inevitable in a different way. There is bias in the nature of technology that tilts it in certain directions and not others. All things being equal, the physics and mathematics that rule the dynamics of technology tend to favor certain behaviors. These tendencies exist primarily in the aggregate forces that shape the general contours of technological forms and do not govern specifics or particular instances.”

“My intent in this book is to uncover the roots of digital change so that we can embrace them. Once seen, we can work with their nature, rather than struggle against it.”

“This is the time when inhabitants of this planet first linked themselves together into one very large thing.”

Title: The Shallows: What the Internet Is Doing to Our Brains
Author: Nicholas Carr
My Review:
We become, neurologically, what we think.

The findings indicated, as van Nimwegen reported, that those using the unhelpful software were better able to plan ahead and plot strategy, while those using the helpful software tended to rely on simple trial and error. Often, in fact, those with the helpful software were found “to aimlessly click around” as they tried to crack the puzzle.

That’s the essence of Kubrick’s dark prophecy: as we come to rely on computers to mediate our understanding of the world, it is our own intelligence that flattens into artificial intelligence.

I do hope it was more believable at times. Adam's character is quite conventional at most times. Nevertheless, it did keep me engaged throughout, I guess that is what matters :)



Tom's character twist felt too convenient?

My Review:
We all make the same kind of mistakes as smokejumpers and firefighters, but the consequences are less dire and therefore often go unnoticed.

A similar pattern emerged in a study of students who graduated at the top of their class. “Valedictorians aren’t likely to be the future’s visionaries,” education researcher Karen Arnold explains. “They typically settle into the system instead of shaking it up.

Title: Outliers: The Story of Success
Author: Malcolm Gladwell
My Review:
Virtually all of the advantage that wealthy students have over poor students is the result of differences in the way privileged kids learn while they are not in school.


Been quite a while since I read any self-help, but just wanted to have a look at this as this seemed to show up everywhere. A book that really lets you think about how success is more about having the strength and presence of mind to seize a given opportunity rather than the opportunity itself. It dives into several rabbithole theories with different case studies, which were actually more interesting than I expected :)

Title: Sapiens: A Brief History of Humankind
Author: Yuval Noah Harari
My Review: How much more interesting can history get!

Title: The Martian
Author: Andy Weir
My Review:
A uniquely realistic piece of drama. I am sure the most appropriate audience for this one would be the science cum tech geeks in an adventurious setup rather than the space nerds.

I almost want to label this as an educational book because of the amount of ‘real things’ you learn about how space travel works: orbial dynamics, EVAs, oxygenerator, rovers, jet propulsion systems and many. Seriously, like, I don't know if there's any other author who takes time to unravel the engineering marvels and equipments at such a fundamental level, afterall for a general audince.

The emotions: they were right at where they have to be, in terms of depth and relatability. This book doesn't try to break the bush, like get too philosophically existential as a means to make a lasting impression, rather it shows real science in a fictitious world (i.e. what science-fiction is of course). I do wish it showed some heavy emotional breakdown of some kind.

The narrative was not very perceivable at very certain situations (mostly in the beginning) which might tend many to re-read. That lack of visual communication is perhaps what many would complain about the book. For example, I did vaguely understand how atmospheric regulators work but didn't get any idea of its shape/body whatsoever.

Who cares though? The story was compelling from beginning to end. The characters were believable and interesting. And... I like the humour.

Title: It Ends with Us (It Ends with Us, #1)
Author: Colleen Hoover
My Review:
One of the those with a very humane & conventional storylines, gradually gives you new perspectives on things. I kind of picked this at random, maybe the cover & genre together got me in a go. It doesn't take you much long to sense that this is apparently a very personal story for Colleen.

As much as I was rooting for almost everybody in this, I so wish that the Ryle's psychological condition was explained a bit more authentically. I guess I couldn't understand on why having a tough childhood justifies his misogony. Besides, one of the primary reasons that's stopping from giving this a solid 4 is it's not consistently engaging, though it picks up during the latter half .
